analogy: Think of "Constitution: Why and How?" through the chapter's own opening images: INTRODUCTION This book is about the working of the Indian Constitution. In the chapters that follow, you will read information about various aspects of the working of our Constitution. You will learn about the various institutions of the government in our country and their relationship... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CONSTITUTION:, WHY AND HOW?, INTRODUCTION, WHY DO WE NEED A CONSTITUTION?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Constitution: Why and How? is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that iNTRODUCTION This book is about the working of the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that in the chapters that follow, you will read information about various aspects of the working of our Constitution.

The text explains that you will learn about the various institutions of the government in our country and their relationship with each other.

The text explains that but before you begin to read about elections, governments, and presidents and prime ministers, it is necessary to understand that the entire structure of the government and the various principles that bind the institutions of government have their origin in the Constitution of India.

The text explains that after studying this chapter, you will learn: ± what a constitution means; ± what a constitution does to the society; ± how constitutions govern the allocation of power in society; and ± what was the way in which the Constitution of India was made.

The text explains that 2 Indian Constitution at Work WHY DO WE NEED A CONSTITUTION?

The text explains that how does a constitution relate to our daily existence?

The text explains that answering these questions is not as difficult as you might think.

The text explains that constitution allows coordination and assurance Imagine yourself to be a member of a reasonably large group.

The text explains that further imagine that this group has the following characteristics.

The text explains that the members of this group are diverse in various ways.

The text explains that they have different religious allegiances: some are Hindus, some are Muslims, some Christians and some perhaps profess no religion at all.

The text explains that they are also varied in many different respects: they pursue different professions, have different abilities, have different hobbies, different tastes in everything from films to books.

The text explains that imagine further that members of this group are likely to have disputes over various aspects of life: How much property should one be allowed to own?

The text explains that should it be compulsory that every child be sent to school or should the parents be allowed to decide?

The text explains that how much should this group spend on its safety and security?

The text explains that every question will elicit a variety of answers from different people.

The text explains that every individual would be insecure simply because they would not know what members of this group could do to each other, who could claim rights over what.

The text explains that if citizens have no assurance that others will follow these rules, they will themselves have no reason to follow these rules.

The text explains that the first function of a constitution is to provide a set of basic rules that allow for minimal coordination amongst members of a society.

The text explains that the decision could be about: ± How would the class representatives be chosen? ± Which decisions will the representative be able to take on behalf of the entire class? ± Are there some decisions that the class representative cannot take without consulting the entire class? ± You can add any other items to this list (collection of common kitty for the class, organisation of picnic and trips, sharing of common resources, …) as long as everyone agrees to it.

The text explains that 4 Indian Constitution at Work ± Write down all these decisions on a paper and put it up on the notice board.

The text explains that specification of decision making powers A constitution is a body of fundamental principles according to which a state is constituted or governed.

The text explains that well, the first question you will have to decide is who gets to decide what the laws governing the society should be?

The text explains that the constitution has to provide an answer to this question.

The text explains that it specifies the basic allocation of power in a society.

The text explains that in principle, this question, who gets to decide, can be answered in many ways: in a monarchical constitution, a monarch decides; in some constitutions like the old Soviet Union, one single party was given the power to decide.

The text explains that but in democratic constitutions, broadly speaking, the people get to decide.

The text explains that because even if you answer that the people should decide, it will not answer the question: how should the people decide?

The text explains that should the people directly vote on each matter as the ancient Greeks did?

The text explains that in the Indian Constitution for example, it is specified that in most instances, Parliament gets to decide laws and policies, and that Parliament itself be organised in a particular manner.

The text explains that before identifying what the law in any given society is, you have to identify

The text explains that 5 Chapter 1: Constitution: Why and How? who has the authority to enact it.

The text explains that it is an authority that constitutes government in the first place.

The text explains that the second function of a constitution is to specify who has the power to make decisions in a society.

The text explains that it decides how the government will be constituted.

The text explains that limitations on the powers of government But this is clearly not enough.

The text explains that or that government could arbitrarily arrest someone, or that only people of a certain skin colour would be allowed to draw water from wells.

The text explains that and even though they were passed by a government that had come into existence based “European Constitution” by Patrick Chappate, International Herald Tribune, 21SEP04 Copyright Cagle Cartoons.

The text explains that rEAD A CARTOON Countries of the European Union tried to create a European constitution.

The text explains that does this always happen in any constitution making?

The text explains that 6 Indian Constitution at Work on certain procedures there would be something obviously unjust about that government enacting these laws.

The text explains that so the third function of a constitution is to set some limits on what a government can impose on its citizens.

The text explains that rajat asked his teacher this question: “ The constitution is a fifty year old and therefore outdated book.

The text explains that it is written in such tough language that I cannot understand it.

The text explains that tell me why should I obey this document?” If you were the teacher, how would you answer Rajat?

The text explains that in a discussion on the experience of the working of our Constitution, three speakers took three different positions: a.

The text explains that harbans: The Indian Constitution has succeeded in giving us a framework of democratic government. b.

The text explains that neha: The Constitution made solemn promises of ensuring liberty, equality and fraternity.

The text explains that since this has not happened, the Constitution has failed. c.

The text explains that you may watch and discuss the Rajya Sabha TV Series, Samvidhaan: The Making of the Constitution of India, and films such as Gandhi, Sardar, and Dr.

The text explains that these audio-visual materials will deepen your understanding of the context in which the Constitution was made.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 1: Constitution: Why and How?

prelims_traps: Do not treat CONSTITUTION: and WHY AND HOW? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Constitution: Why and How?; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Constitution: Why and How? through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CONSTITUTION: and WHY AND HOW? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
